metadata.title: Guideline on Clinical Investigation of Medicinal Products Indicated for the Treatment of Psoriasis

rules.rule_text: The guideline focuses on chronic plaque psoriasis. Severe forms such as psoriatic erythroderma, generalised pustular psoriasis, and psoriatic arthritis are outside the scope, though general principles may be extrapolated.
rules.verbatim_quote: This guidance will focus mainly on development of topical and systemic treatments of chronic plaque psoriasis. Although general principles of this guidance can be extrapolated to the evaluation of drugs indicated for severe forms of psoriasis with systemic impairment, (psoriatic erythroderma, generalised pustular psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis), these forms are not within the scope of this guideline.
rules.exceptions: General principles may be extrapolated to severe systemic forms

rules.rule_text: A washout period is required for previously treated patients in short-term pivotal trials. The washout must cover any drug that may improve or worsen psoriasis.
rules.verbatim_quote: In previously treated patients, a washout period is necessary in order to properly evaluate the effect of tested drug in short-term pivotal trials. The wash-out should be considered for any drug that may improve or worsen psoriasis.

rules.rule_text: The washout period duration must account for pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of prior therapy and must be carefully considered to avoid unnecessary rebound of psoriasis.
rules.verbatim_quote: The duration of the washout period should take into account pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of previously applied therapy; in addition, it has to be carefully considered in order to avoid unnecessary rebound of psoriasis.

rules.rule_text: A placebo arm is required to ascertain study sensitivity due to the seasonal and fluctuating character of psoriasis. Topical treatments require topical placebo (vehicle); systemic treatments require systemic placebo.
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to a seasonal and fluctuating character of the disease, placebo arm is needed to ascertain study sensitivity: for topical treatments, topical placebo (usually test vehicle), for systemic treatments, systemic placebo.
rules.exceptions: When the test vehicle is clearly irritant, emollient should be used in the control arm instead

rules.rule_text: Background care (emollients, shampoos) must be standardised across treatment arms in psoriasis trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: The background care (emollients, shampoos) should be standardised.

rules.rule_text: Blinding should be systematically performed in topical trials versus vehicle. Where blinding is compromised, an independent blinded evaluation of response to treatment must be performed, potentially using standardised photographs reviewed by an independent third party.
rules.verbatim_quote: However, even in cases where blinding is compromised, it is recommended to perform at least independent blinded evaluation of response to treatment. A blinded evaluation of standardised photographs, reviewed by an independent third party, may be helpful.
rules.exceptions: Certain agents (tazarotene, dioxyanthranol) may compromise blinding; with systemic agents blinding may be difficult (retinoids side effects, PUVA therapy)

rules.rule_text: Inter-individual comparison with parallel-groups is the recommended study design for therapeutic confirmatory trials. Intra-individual (within-patient, left-right) comparisons must be restricted to therapeutic exploratory trials only.
rules.verbatim_quote: inter-individual comparison with parallel-groups is the recommended study design in therapeutic confirmatory trials.
rules.exceptions: Intra-individual comparisons acceptable for exploratory trials evaluating short-term changes

rules.rule_text: Trial duration of 8 to 12 weeks (4 weeks for potent topical corticosteroids) is generally sufficient to show short-term efficacy. Pre-specified assessment time points must include at minimum 0, 4, 8 and 12 weeks where applicable.
rules.verbatim_quote: A duration of 8 to 12 weeks (4 weeks for a potent topically applied corticosteroid) is generally sufficient to show short-term efficacy, except for drugs with slow onset of action, where longer study duration may be needed. Pre-specified time points for assessment should include as a minimum 0, 4, 8 and 12 weeks, where applicable.
rules.exceptions: Drugs with slow onset of action may need longer duration

rules.rule_text: At least one therapeutic confirmatory trial must include a post-treatment observation period of at least 2 months where responders are randomised to active drug or placebo to explore duration of remission/response, rebound and time to relapse.
rules.verbatim_quote: In at least one therapeutic confirmatory trial, the initial study period may be followed by an observation period of at least 2 months where responders to treatment are randomised either to active drug or to placebo in order to explore the duration of remission/response, rebound and time to relapse.

rules.rule_text: One-year intermittent or prolonged use safety and efficacy studies are recommended for psoriasis as a chronic relapsing disease. Long-term safety data beyond 1 year may be necessary depending on the agent.
rules.verbatim_quote: Psoriasis is a seasonal, chronic relapsing disease and one-year intermittent or prolonged use (as appropriate) safety and efficacy studies are recommended. Depending on the agent, long-term safety data beyond 1 year may be necessary.

rules.rule_text: For registration, studies must evaluate not only response to treatment but also durability of remission/response, relapse and rebound after end of treatment. This is a minimal requirement.
rules.verbatim_quote: The indication "treatment of mild/moderate/severe psoriasis" may be granted in case of positive results obtained in studies evaluating not only the response to treatment in the target population but also the durability of remission/response, relapse and rebound after the end of treatment. This is a minimal requirement for registration.

rules.rule_text: If there is evidence of effect attenuation with repeated use, sustained efficacy during the second treatment period must be demonstrated.
rules.verbatim_quote: A sustained efficacy during the second treatment period may have to be demonstrated in cases where there is evidence that effect attenuation may occur with repeated use.
rules.exceptions: Especially important for drugs administered in well-defined cycles

rules.rule_text: Target population description must include psoriasis type, disease duration, extent/severity, and number/type of previous treatments to ensure population homogeneity.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is of major importance for the applicant to describe: - the type of psoriasis (e.g. chronic plaque or palmo-plantar, etc) - disease duration - the extent/severity of psoriasis, (one of the main predictors of treatment response) - the number/type of previous treatments, if any

rules.rule_text: Pivotal trials should include homogeneous populations: mild to moderate psoriasis for topical agents, moderate to severe psoriasis for systemic agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is generally recommended to include homogenous population in pivotal trials: e.g. mild to moderate psoriasis for topical agents, and moderate to severe psoriasis for systemic agents.

rules.rule_text: Special psoriasis locations (scalp, nails, palms and soles) must be evaluated separately using their own scoring systems, as they show different treatment response behaviour from total body psoriasis.
rules.verbatim_quote: These areas often show different behaviour with respect to response to treatment and therefore need special observation; in addition, scoring systems different from "total body" psoriasis scoring systems are used. Therefore, they should better be evaluated separately.

rules.rule_text: Two endpoints must be used to assess efficacy: a validated, standardised global score (e.g. PGA) in conjunction with PASI. PASI alone is not sufficient.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is considered that PASI alone is not sufficient to evaluate psoriasis severity at baseline and on treatment. Therefore, it is strongly recommended to use two endpoints to assess efficacy: a validated, standardised global score (e.g. PGA) in conjunction with PASI.

rules.rule_text: The primary efficacy endpoint should be the percentage of patients achieving 'clear or almost clear' (PASI >90%) on treatment. In severe patients, PASI >75% may also define responders if prospectively defined.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, the best evidence of efficacy is the percentage of patients who achieve the result of "clear or almost clear" (PASI>90%) on treatment. In studies enrolling severe patients, patients who achieve the result of "mild" (PASI>75%) may also be considered as responders if defined prospectively.
rules.exceptions: PASI 50 may be acceptable as a secondary endpoint for literature comparison; a reduction in PASI score of <=50% is not considered acceptable demonstration of treatment response

rules.rule_text: Response to treatment must be documented as difference between baseline and post-treatment scores of both BSA affected and the three main skin signs (erythema, scale, elevation).
rules.verbatim_quote: Response to treatment should be documented as the difference between baseline and post-treatment score of both body surface area affected with psoriasis and the three main skin signs (erythema, scale and elevation).

rules.rule_text: Visual assessment of index lesions must include at least 2 index lesions representative of the disease (one from refractory area such as elbow or knee, one from trunk).
rules.verbatim_quote: Visual assessment of index lesions: measurements of at least 2 index lesions representative of the disease (one from refractory area - elbow or knee, and one from trunk) for separate variables including erythema, scale and elevation on a 3-point scales.

rules.rule_text: Treatment success is defined as patient clear or almost clear on a global scale, or >90% improvement in PASI from baseline. Remission is complete clearing (residual post-inflammatory pigmentary alteration not considered residual disease).
rules.source_section: 4.1.1 Additional efficacy measures in short-term trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Treatment success: patient clear or almost clear on a global scale, or >90% improvement in PASI from baseline.

rules.rule_text: Relapse is defined as reduction of achieved maximal improvement from baseline by >50%. Rebound is defined as worsening of psoriasis over baseline value (PASI>125%) or new pustular, erythrodermic or more inflammatory psoriasis occurring within 2 months of stopping therapy.
rules.source_section: 4.1.1 Additional efficacy measures in short-term trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Rebound is defined as worsening of psoriasis over baseline value (e.g. PASI>125%) or new pustular, erythrodermic or more inflammatory psoriasis occurring within 2 months of stopping therapy.
rules.exceptions: Simple worsening beyond 2 months may represent natural course (relapse) rather than drug-associated rebound

rules.rule_text: HRQL scales used in psoriasis trials must be previously validated. Psoriasis-specific HRQL should assess patient's perspective to understand clinical significance of benefit.
rules.verbatim_quote: The previous validation of HRQL scales is mandatory.

rules.rule_text: For topical agents, PK studies on repeated applications must compare healthy skin versus psoriatic skin and evaluate systemic exposure/BSA, skin reservoir effects, PK in children and elderly, and PK in different skin sites.
rules.verbatim_quote: PK on repeated applications on healthy skin versus psoriatic skin - systemic exposure / BSA - effect on "skin reservoir" on frequency of applications - PK in children and in elderly - PK in different skin sites (ex. flexures versus non-flexural areas)

rules.rule_text: Double-blind vehicle-controlled dose-response studies must be performed for topical agents, testing several drug concentrations and establishing optimal frequency of application.
rules.verbatim_quote: Double-blind vehicle-controlled dose-response studies should be performed to determine the efficacy of the active drug. In general, several drug concentrations should be tested. Moreover, optimal frequency of application should be clearly established.

rules.rule_text: Therapeutic confirmatory trials for topical agents must be double-blind, vehicle-controlled, parallel-group studies showing clinically meaningful superiority over vehicle.
rules.verbatim_quote: Therapeutic confirmatory trials should be double-blind, vehicle-controlled, parallel-group studies showing a clinically meaningful superiority of a new drug to its vehicle.
rules.exceptions: If vehicle is irritant, comparison to emollient cream may be needed

rules.rule_text: For active comparator studies with topical agents, three-arm trials (new agent, active comparator, vehicle) are recommended. Vehicle arm is needed to ascertain assay sensitivity. Non-inferiority margin must be prospectively defined.
rules.verbatim_quote: Parallel group, double-blind, vehicle and active comparator controlled studies are recommended. Only three-arm trials allow comparison of the efficacy and safety of a new agent both with the vehicle and an active comparator... a non-inferiority margin should be prospectively defined.
rules.exceptions: Two-arm trials acceptable for superiority claims only; non-inferiority cannot be claimed without placebo arm

rules.rule_text: For systemic agents, both men and women with psoriasis for at least 12 months must be investigated. Exclusion of specific patient groups must be justified.
rules.source_section: 5.2.3 Study population and selection of patients
rules.verbatim_quote: Both men and women with psoriasis for at least 12 months should be investigated. The exclusion of specific patient groups should be justified.

rules.rule_text: Dose-finding studies for systemic agents must be randomised, placebo-controlled, parallel group design, using at least 3 dosages. Fixed doses vs body-weight-related doses must be evaluated.
rules.verbatim_quote: The dose-finding studies should be randomised, and placebo-controlled using parallel group design. The studies should characterise the most beneficial part of the dose-response curve and use at least 3 dosages to establish the optimal dose compared to placebo. The feasibility of fixed doses or need for body weight related doses should be evaluated.

rules.rule_text: Three-armed parallel-group confirmatory studies with active agent, placebo and comparative active treatment are strongly recommended for systemic agents. Non-inferiority margin must be prospectively defined based on established comparator efficacy over placebo.
rules.verbatim_quote: A three-armed, parallel-group studies with the active agent, placebo and comparative active treatment are strongly recommended. In order to assure the best possible assessment of the effect size of a new agent, the non-inferiority margin should be prospectively defined by taking into account the established efficacy of the chosen comparator over placebo.

rules.rule_text: The approvability of a new systemic drug for severe plaque psoriasis supported only by placebo-controlled studies (without active comparator) can be seriously questioned.
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, the approvability of a new drug for the treatment of severe plaque psoriasis, supported by efficacy results of placebo controlled studies only, can be seriously questioned.
rules.exceptions: Placebo-controlled studies alone may be acceptable in patients with contraindications to all existing systemic treatments, but this results in restricted indication

rules.rule_text: For systemic confirmatory trials, follow-up of 3 to 6 months after cessation of therapy is required to evaluate time to initial and maximal response, duration of remission/efficacy, time to relapse, and rebound.
rules.verbatim_quote: evaluation of efficacy at the end of short-term treatment (8-12 weeks) and during a follow-up of 3 to 6 months after cessation of therapy is needed. Since one of the major goals of therapy is to maintain response/remission in responders, time of response/remission, time to relapse and rebound phenomena should be studied during the follow-up.

rules.rule_text: Combination therapy trials must demonstrate advantage over monotherapy through additive efficacy, better tolerance, or reduction of cumulative systemic exposure. A four-arm trial design (combination, each agent alone, placebo/vehicle) is the suitable design.
rules.verbatim_quote: A four-arm trial comparing combination therapy to each agent alone and to placebo(vehicle) is a suitable study design.
cross_references: EU Fixed combination medicinal products guidance

rules.rule_text: In children and during pregnancy with severe chronic plaque psoriasis, topical treatments are preferred over systemic ones due to toxicity. Therapeutic trials in pregnant women are not performed for topically applied agents due to possible systemic passage.
rules.verbatim_quote: Both in children and during pregnancy, even in cases with severe forms of chronic plaque psoriasis, topical treatments are preferred to systemic ones due to toxicity. However, due to a possible systemic passage of topically applied agents, therapeutic trials are not performed in pregnant women.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric studies for safety concerns must focus on long-term potential systemic effects, especially in very young children (<5 years): cellular and antibody-mediated immunity, hormonal status, growth and skeletal changes, calcium blood levels.
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of safety concerns, paediatric studies should specifically focus on long-term potential systemic effects, especially in very young children (<5 years), i.e. on cellular and antibody-mediated immunity, hormonal status, growth and skeletal changes, calcium blood levels, etc.
rules.exceptions: If no particular safety concerns, specific paediatric studies in plaque psoriasis are not warranted

rules.rule_text: Long-term safety data beyond 1 year are required for psoriasis as a chronic condition, especially for new systemic agents with novel pharmacological mechanisms. Post-approval long-term surveillance studies may be mandated, focusing on carcinogenic effects, immunosuppressive effects, infections, and auto-immunity induction.
rules.verbatim_quote: Long-term safety is of primary importance in psoriasis as this is a chronic condition and safety data beyond 1 year are required (See ICH topic E1 on population exposure to assess clinical safety), especially for new systemic agents, based on new pharmacological mechanisms. These agents may be subjected to post-approval long-term surveillance studies.
